one_line_takeaway: A survey (not a new method) that is the authors' claimed first comprehensive review of audiovisual/multimodal deepfake DETECTION, contributing a taxonomy of methods and datasets plus a human-perception angle, and identifying cross-modal synchronization as the key cue and generalization as the key open problem.
key_finding: The survey's synthesized takeaways: (1) jointly modeling audio and visual streams outperforms unimodal detection, with cross-modal / synchronization inconsistency (e.g. lip-sync, phoneme-viseme, modality dissonance) being the dominant detection signal; (2) generalization is the central unsolved problem -- audiovisual detectors perform well on seen datasets/manipulations but degrade sharply on unseen ones and on in-the-wild degradations (compression, occlusion, non-frontal faces, noise, multiple speakers); (3) humans are surprisingly vulnerable to high-quality deepfakes and AI detectors generally surpass human perception, yet human contextual intuition is complementary and worth integrating; (4) scalability/real-time bimodal processing and privacy/ethics of collecting facial and voice data are open challenges.
core_contribution: This is a SURVEY (literature review), not a new detector. Its own contribution is organizational: the authors position it as the first comprehensive review dedicated specifically to MULTIMODAL (audiovisual) deepfake DETECTION, bridging the unimodal-vs-multimodal gap. Concretely it delivers (1) a taxonomy of deepfakes (text/audio/visual/audiovisual) and of audiovisual video categories (FVFA, RVFA, FVRA, RVRA); (2) a classification of audiovisual detection methods into five families -- synchronization-based, feature-fusion, ensemble, temporal-analysis-based, and 'other'; (3) a consolidated summary of five public audiovisual datasets with statistics; (4) an integration of human-perception / psychological-factor findings alongside the algorithmic methods; and (5) a challenges-and-future-directions roadmap. IMPORTANT: the named detection systems it discusses (e.g. Multimodaltrace, AVFF, AVT2-DWF, AVFakeNet, AVTENet, BA-TFD/BA-TFD+, AVoiD-DF, MDS, DF-TransFusion) are CITED third-party works being reviewed, NOT methods proposed by this survey. Several are the authors' own prior papers (Lip Sync Matters [18], ensemble learning [19], AVTENet [123], human-perception study [146]) but are still cited works, not new contributions of this survey.
threat_model: The general deepfake threat landscape the survey defends against (it proposes no adversary model of its own). Adversaries use GAN/VAE/TTS/voice-cloning tools -- now accessible even to non-experts -- to fabricate audio, video, or joint audiovisual content for political/religious impersonation, disinformation and fake news, defamation of celebrities/public figures, revenge porn, fraud and identity theft, and erosion of trust in journalism and photographic/audio evidence. The survey's motivating premise is that BIMODAL (audio+visual) manipulation makes fakes more convincing and harder to detect than single-modality forgeries, and that detectors exploiting cross-modal / audio-visual synchronization inconsistencies are the countermeasure.
limitations: Acknowledged by the paper: generalization gap to unseen manipulations; detectors trained on clean, preprocessed, gender/racially biased datasets; heavy compute for real-time two-stream processing (calls for pruning/quantization/distillation); privacy and consent concerns around facial/speech biometric data. Review-level limitations I note: as a survey it presents NO quantitative benchmark comparison of the reviewed detectors (Section VI gives only metric definitions, no results table), so all relative-efficacy claims about surveyed methods are qualitative and second-hand from the cited papers. There is also an internal inconsistency in the DFDC statistics -- the prose cites 119,154 full-set videos / 486 (or 3,426 paid) subjects, while Table II lists DFDC as 960 subjects, 23,654 real, 104,500 fake, 128,154 total.

quantitative_results: These are DATASET statistics from the survey's Table II, NOT detection-accuracy numbers (the survey reports no detection performance of its own).

DFDC (2021 label in text, Table lists 2019): 960 subjects, 23,654 real / 104,500 fake / 128,154 total videos (prose alternatively cites 119,154 full-set videos, 10s each, 486 unique subjects, 3,426 paid subjects, 5,214-video preview) -- audiovisual manipulation
FakeAVCeleb: 500 subjects, 500 real / 19,500 fake / 20,000 total (500 originals ~30s each; 470 subjects train / 70 test) -- audiovisual manipulation
LAV-DF: 153 subjects, 36,431 real / 99,873 fake / 136,304 total -- content-driven reenactment + TTS, with forgery localization
AV-Deepfake1M: 2,068 subjects (2000+), 286,721 real / 860,039 fake / 1,146,760 total (>1M videos) -- LLM-driven audiovisual
AV-PolyGlotFake: subjects not reported, 766 real / 14,472 fake / 15,238 total (>15,000 videos), 7 languages
baselines_compared: Not applicable in the experimental sense -- this is a survey with no experiments or baselines.

Table I positions this survey against seven prior deepfake survey papers: Verdoliva 2020 [25], Mirsky et al. 2021 [26], Yu et al. 2021 [27], Rana et al. 2022 [28], Nguyen et al. 2022 [29], Masood et al. 2023 [30], Mubarak et al. 2023 [31], arguing none focuses on audiovisual/multimodal detection.
